content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 14, 1976

W. A. McCormick, Controller
The Republican Central Committee
of Los Angeles County
1326 West 6th Street
Los Angeles, CA 90017

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 75-214

Dear Mr. McCormick:

After accepting your opinion request of December 2,
1975, the Commission adopted regulations clarifying the meaning of the terms "contribution" and "expenditure." 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Sections 18215, 18225. Because the questions you
posed can be answered by reference to those regulations, the
Commission has decided not to adopt a formal opinion. Instead,
this letter responds to your request.

Your first question concerned monthly luncheon meetings held by Republican Women's Clubs. Each member of the
club treasury a check for $3.00 to $5.00 to cover the cost of
the meal and may include an additional $.10 to $.25 to pay the
luncheon costs of a speaker. The club treasurer replaces 25
to 75 individual checks with a single check, drawn on the club
account and made payable to the restaurant. Under these circumstances, a member of a Republican Women's Club has not made
a contribution to the club because the member has received
full and adequate consideration in exchange for the payment
she makes. Government Code Section 82015. The club treasurer
merely simplifies the restaurant's bookkeeping by collecting
checks from all the members and making a single payment to the
restaurant. Moreover, including an additional $.10 to $.25 to
cover the luncheon costs of a speaker does not change our
conclusion. The speech is full and adequate consideration in
exchange for the additional $.10 to $.25 paid by each member.

You also asked a number of questions pertaining to
club fundraising activities. One fundraising activity is a
rummage sale where club members and the general public purchase rummage that has been donated by the members. You asked
whether the donated rummage is a "contribution" and whether
proceeds from the rummage sale are reportable as "contributions."

W. A. McCormick
September 14, 1976
Page Two


     If the club holds a rummage sale to raise money in
support of its ongoing activities, the club has no reporting
obligations. Monetary and nonmonetary donations received by
the club are "contributions" within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act only if the donations are made for political purposes,
that is if the payments are "earmarked for the making of contri-
butions or expenditures." Section 82015, 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18215(d).1/  For the payments to be "earmarked" the
donor must know or have reason to know that all or a portion
of the donation will be used to make contributions or expenditures.

     You stated that the clubs hold rummage sales to
raise money in support of their ongoing activities, and that
you are unaware of a single instance when a sale has been held
to raise money for the making of contributions and expenditures.
Given these facts, neither the donated rummage nor the proceeds
from the sale are "contributions" within the meaning of Section
82015 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215.     Consequently, a
Republican club incurs no reporting obligations as a result of
such a rummage sale.

     Other forms of fundraising activities engaged in by
the clubs include periodic sales of political books where the
profit realized is 25% to 35% of the sales price and jewelry
sales by the Federated Women's Clubs at which the jewelry is
marked up 25% to 35%. You asked whether the wholesale price
paid by the club to obtain the books and jewelry is reportable
as an expenditure. In addition, you inquired whether all or
any portion of the retail price is reportable as a contribution.

     The sale of books or jewelry will not impose report-
ing requirements on the clubs unless the sale is held to raise
money that will be used to make contributions or expenditures.
You stated that the clubs sell books and jewelry to raise

                              1/
     Any payment for which full and adequate considera-
tion is not made to the donor that is received by a candidate,
controlled committee, official committee of a political party,
or organization formed or existing primarily for political
purposes is a contribution. The Republican clubs do not fall
within any of these categories. They are not official commit-
tees of a political party and are not organized for the major
purpose of electing or defeating candidates and ballot measures.
Instead, they exist to provide a forum for discussing issues, a
place to socialize and a means to perform community services,
as well as a mechanism for supporting candidates and measures.
Consequently, donations received by political clubs are contri-
butions only if they are "'earmarked' for the making of expen-
ditures and contributions." See Opinion requested by Francis
Willmarth, 2 FPPC Opinions 130 (No. 75-188, August 3, 1976), a
copy of which is enclosed.

W. A. McCormick
September 14, 1976
Page Three


money in support of their ongoing activities, not to generate
funds for the support of candidates or measures. Therefore,
the wholesale costs of the books and jewelry are not expendi-
tures and revenues obtained from the sales are not contri-
butions. Hence, a club does not incur any reporting obliga-
tions as a result of the sales.

     I hope this information will be of assistance. If
you have any further questions, please contact Natalie B.
West, an attorney with the Commission's legal staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:NEW:plh
Enclosure

THE REPUBLICAN
Central Committee of Los Angeles County, Rep. Since 12-22-?

J. WILLIAM OROZCO
CHAIRMAN

483-3550
1326 WEST 6TH STREET
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017

2 December 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 897
Sacramento, California, 95804

Gentlemen,

As controller of the Republican Central Committee of Los Angeles County, I am the adviser for nearly 90 reporting clubs of the Federation of Republican Women. 40 independent Republican clubs in the county, and for all young Republican clubs in the county large enough to qualify, reporting-wise, under Proposition Nine. All have the same basic problem, in varying degrees, and they have requested that I obtain a quick clarification of that problem.

The women's federated clubs hold monthly meetings in restaurants in their own areas. Each member gives check to her-club treasurer for the cost of the meal where the meeting is held; the treasurer replaces 25-75 individual checks with one federated check made payable to the restaurant. Some clubs charge 10¢-25¢ more than actual cost in order to fund the luncheon costs of the speakers or the guests. Does such a monthly check of $3 - $5 become a part of the cumulative contributions of the members?

The federated clubs, the independent clubs, and even some of the young Republican clubs hold rummage sales where club members purchase as well as the public at large. Conceded that the revenue must be reported, should it be considered as a donation or may it be classified as a miscellaneous receipt? In other words, must itemization be maintained on all transactions? How must the cost of the rummage be handled?

All the above-mentioned clubs may hold periodic sales of political books where the profit realized is 25%-35% of the sales price. In addition, the federated clubs continue to offer political and/or patriotic jewelry for sale at a 25%-35% markup. Do all these revenues qualify under the miscellaneous receipts category? Cost of the books and the jewelry has always been included in the expenditures of the club.

Because the size of the clubs vary from 10 to 500 members, details of the aforementioned transactions may be quite cumbersome. I have previously submitted a copy of a women's federated club report where the monthly luncheons, dues, and fashion shows in the first half of the year 1975 made more than 50% of the members reportable as itemized donors of $50 or more if miscellaneous receipts reporting is not permitted.

Thank you very much for your prompt consideration of the problems cited above.

Sincerely,

[illegible] Carmick
W.A. McCormick, Controller

MAY: hs
